home.title: Mathematics — Revision
home.intro: Five topics covering the G9 EOY revision list, with Dr Frost practice and interactive simulations.
home.examStructureBody: Full e-assessment on AssessPrep. Four criteria, 25 marks each (100 total). Criterion A — Knowing & Understanding (short calculations). Criteria B & D — one extended task each, with several sub-parts (B = investigating patterns, D = real-life context). Criterion C — Communication runs across all sections (show working, units, conclusion). Past EOY 1 (Y9636Y) tested orientation in space and time (Mars data, factory); EOY 2 (GD29PC) tested personal/cultural expression (Rangoli, Mandala). Long Criterion B/D tasks always include: identify factors → calculate → labelled sketch → justify.

extras.title: Mathematics — Extras
extras.intro: Topics that extend beyond the G9 revision list — useful for IB DP preparation or for curious students.

calculus.title: Calculus — derivatives intro
calculus.body: A derivative measures the rate of change of a function. For y = xⁿ, the derivative is dy/dx = n·xⁿ⁻¹. The derivative of a constant is zero. Geometric meaning: slope of the tangent at a point. Used in physics (velocity = derivative of position) and optimisation (maximum/minimum problems).

proofs.title: Mathematical proof
items: Direct proof — assume premise, derive conclusion step by step.
Proof by contradiction — assume opposite, show it leads to absurdity.
Proof by induction — base case + inductive step (n → n+1). Used for sums and divisibility.
Counterexample — one example disproves a 'for all' claim.

direct.name: Direct proof
direct.rule: Start from the GIVEN premises and use logical steps (definitions, axioms, theorems) to derive the conclusion. Most common type. Best when there's a clear path.
examples: PROVE: sum of two even numbers is even. Let a = 2m, b = 2n (definition of even). a + b = 2m + 2n = 2(m+n). 2(m+n) is even (definition). QED.
PROVE: angles in a triangle sum to 180°. Draw a line through one vertex parallel to the opposite side. Use alternate angles. Done.
PROVE: a² − b² = (a−b)(a+b). Expand right side: (a−b)(a+b) = a² + ab − ab − b² = a² − b². QED.
direct.tip: Always state what you're TRYING to prove at the top. End with QED (or ∎) to signal the proof is complete.

contradiction.name: Proof by contradiction
contradiction.rule: Assume the OPPOSITE of what you want to prove. Derive a logical contradiction (something obviously false). Conclude original statement must be true. Powerful when direct proof is awkward.
examples: PROVE: √2 is irrational. Assume √2 IS rational, so √2 = a/b in lowest terms. Square: 2 = a²/b². So a² = 2b². a² is even → a is even → a = 2k. Then 4k² = 2b² → b² = 2k² → b also even. But a/b was in lowest terms — CONTRADICTION. So √2 is irrational.

PROVE: there are infinitely many primes. Assume finitely many: p₁, p₂, …, pₙ. Consider N = p₁·p₂·…·pₙ + 1. N is not divisible by any pᵢ (remainder 1). So either N is prime (contradicts list being complete) or has a new prime factor.
contradiction.watchOut: When deriving contradiction, BE EXPLICIT about what contradicts what. Don't just say 'this is wrong' — show why.

induction.name: Proof by induction
induction.rule: Two steps: (1) BASE CASE — show statement true for n=1. (2) INDUCTIVE STEP — assume true for n=k, show true for n=k+1. Conclusion: true for all positive integers. Like dominoes.
examples: PROVE: 1 + 2 + … + n = n(n+1)/2. Base: n=1, LHS=1, RHS=1·2/2=1. ✓ Inductive: assume true for k. Add (k+1): k(k+1)/2 + (k+1) = (k+1)(k+2)/2. ✓
PROVE: 2ⁿ > n for all positive integers. Base: 2¹=2>1. ✓ Inductive: 2^(k+1) = 2·2ᵏ > 2k ≥ k+1 (since k ≥ 1). ✓
Divisibility example: PROVE 7ⁿ − 1 is divisible by 6. Base: 7¹−1 = 6. ✓ Inductive: 7^(k+1) − 1 = 7·7ᵏ − 1 = 7(7ᵏ − 1) + 6 = 7·6m + 6 = 6(7m+1). ✓
induction.tip: Always do BOTH steps explicitly. Skipping the base case is a common error — the inductive step alone proves nothing.

counterexample.name: Counterexample
counterexample.rule: To DISPROVE a 'for all' statement, find a SINGLE example where it fails. To prove a 'for all' statement, you need a proof; to disprove, ONE counter is enough.
examples: CLAIM: 'all primes are odd'. Counterexample: 2 is prime AND even. Disproved.
CLAIM: 'n² > n for all positive integers'. Counterexample: n=1 gives 1²=1, NOT > 1. Disproved.
CLAIM: 'every quadratic has real roots'. Counterexample: x² + 1 = 0 has no real roots. Disproved.
CLAIM: 'if a²=b² then a=b'. Counterexample: a=−3, b=3. Both square to 9 but aren't equal.
counterexample.tip: Look at extreme cases (n=0, n=1, n very large, negative numbers, zero). Most counterexamples come from edge cases.

numberTheory.title: Number theory basics
numberTheory.body: Prime numbers: divisible only by 1 and themselves. There are infinitely many primes (Euclid). Fundamental theorem of arithmetic: every integer >1 has a unique prime factorisation. Modular arithmetic: 17 mod 5 = 2. Useful for divisibility tests and cryptography.

complex.title: Complex numbers — preview
complex.body: i² = −1. Complex number z = a + bi has real part a and imaginary part b. Solves quadratics with negative discriminant: x² + 1 = 0 → x = ±i. Used in electricity, signal processing, quantum physics. You'll meet these in IB DP.

statsExt.title: Statistics extension
statsExt.body: Variance σ² is the average squared deviation from the mean. Standard deviation σ = √variance. Sample vs population: divide by n−1 for sample (Bessel's correction). Correlation coefficient r measures linear relationship (−1 to +1). Scatter plots show variable relationships.

numberSystems.title: Number Systems & Arithmetic
numberSystems.intro: Classification of numbers, set notation, rounding, surds, exponents, standard form and absolute values.

structureSets.name: Structure & Sets — classification, set notation
structureSets.rule: ℕ (naturals: 1,2,3…) ⊂ ℤ (integers: …−2,−1,0,1,2…) ⊂ ℚ (rationals: a/b, b≠0) ⊂ ℝ (reals, including irrationals). Set notation: ∈ (element of), ∪ (union), ∩ (intersection), ⊂ (subset), Ø (empty set).
structureSets.tip: π and e are real but not rational. Surds (√2, √3) are also irrational. Recurring decimals ARE rational.

rounding.name: Rounding & accuracy
rounding.rule: Round to n decimal places (d.p.) or n significant figures (s.f.). Look at the next digit: ≥5 round up, <5 round down. Significant figures: count from the first non-zero digit.
examples: 1250 → 1300 (2 s.f.); 1250 (3 s.f. — could be either depending on rule).
rounding.tip: S.F. is harder. The first non-zero digit is sig fig #1. 0.00408 has 3 s.f. (4, 0, 8 — the middle 0 counts because it's between non-zeros).

bounds.name: Upper and lower bounds (Extended)
bounds.rule: A value rounded to nearest unit has bounds ±½ unit. Lower bound = stated − ½ × precision; Upper bound = stated + ½ × precision.
examples: For maximum velocity = max(distance) / min(time). For min velocity = min(distance) / max(time).
bounds.watchOut: UB is NEVER reached — it's a strict supremum. Use it inclusively in calculations, but it's still 27.5 not 27.499…

exponents.name: Laws of exponents
exponents.rule: aᵐ · aⁿ = aᵐ⁺ⁿ ; aᵐ / aⁿ = aᵐ⁻ⁿ ; (aᵐ)ⁿ = aᵐⁿ ; (ab)ⁿ = aⁿbⁿ ; a⁰ = 1 ; a⁻ⁿ = 1/aⁿ.
exponents.watchOut: These laws only work for the SAME base. 2³ · 3² is NOT 6⁵. Negative exponents flip to fraction, don't make negative number.

fractionalIndices.name: Fractional indices (Extended)
fractionalIndices.rule: a^(1/n) = ⁿ√a (the nth root). a^(m/n) = (ⁿ√a)ᵐ = ⁿ√(aᵐ).
fractionalIndices.tip: Denominator is the ROOT (n in ⁿ√), numerator is the POWER (m in xᵐ). Easier to take root first, then power.

standardForm.name: Standard form (scientific notation)
standardForm.rule: Write as a × 10ⁿ where 1 ≤ a < 10 and n is an integer. Used for very large or very small numbers.
standardForm.watchOut: 5.4 × 10⁵ ≠ 54 × 10⁴ in standard form — `a` must be between 1 and 10. Add/subtract: convert to same power first.

unitConversion.name: Conversion of units
unitConversion.rule: Length: 1 km = 1000 m = 10⁵ cm = 10⁶ mm. Mass: 1 t = 1000 kg = 10⁶ g. Volume: 1 m³ = 1000 L; 1 L = 1000 cm³ = 1000 mL. For compound units, convert each: km/h → m/s requires ÷3.6.
unitConversion.tip: For area, the conversion is squared (m²→cm² ×10⁴). For volume, cubed (m³→cm³ ×10⁶). Common mistake.

surdsSimplify.name: Surds — simplifying
surdsSimplify.rule: A surd is √n where n is not a perfect square. Simplify by extracting the largest perfect-square factor: √(a²b) = a√b.
surdsSimplify.tip: Always find the LARGEST perfect square factor. List of perfect squares: 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81, 100…

surdsOperate.name: Surds — operations & rationalising
surdsOperate.rule: Add/subtract: only LIKE surds combine (3√2 + 5√2 = 8√2). Multiply: √a · √b = √(ab). Divide & rationalise: multiply top & bottom by surd in denominator.

absolute.name: Absolute values
absolute.rule: |x| = distance from 0. Always ≥ 0. |x| = x if x≥0, |x| = −x if x<0. |a − b| = distance between a and b.
absolute.tip: For inequalities: |x| < k → −k < x < k. |x| > k → x > k or x < −k.

functionsAlgebra.title: Functions & Algebra
functionsAlgebra.intro: Mappings, domain/range, quadratics in three forms, function transformations, algebraic fractions, variation.

subTopics.rearrange: Changing the subject of a formula

mappings.name: Mappings, domain & range
mappings.rule: A function maps each input (domain) to exactly one output (range/codomain). Notation: f: x → 2x+1 or f(x) = 2x+1.
examples: If f(x) = 3x − 2, then f(4) = 10 and f(−1) = −5.
mappings.tip: Domain = allowed inputs. Range = actual outputs. Find restrictions: division by 0, even roots of negatives.

verticalLine.name: Vertical line test
verticalLine.rule: A graph represents a function if and only if every vertical line crosses it AT MOST ONCE. If a vertical line crosses twice, one x gives two y's — not a function.
examples: x = y² (sideways parabola) FAILS — vertical line at x=1 hits y=1 and y=−1.
Circle x² + y² = 4 FAILS — not a function.
verticalLine.tip: Quick test for graphs. For algebra, ensure each x has unique y.

quadraticsFactor.name: Quadratics — expand & factorize
quadraticsFactor.rule: Expand: (x+a)(x+b) = x² + (a+b)x + ab. Factorize: find p, q such that p·q = c and p+q = b in x² + bx + c.
quadraticsFactor.tip: Check by expanding back. Difference of squares: a² − b² = (a−b)(a+b) — memorise.

quadraticsGraph.name: Quadratics — graphical representation
quadraticsGraph.rule: y = ax² + bx + c is a parabola. If a > 0, opens up (min); if a < 0, opens down (max). y-intercept = c. x-intercepts where y = 0.
examples: y = x² − 4x + 3 opens up; y-int = 3; x-ints at x=1, x=3.
y = −x² + 4 opens down; y-int = 4; x-ints at x = ±2.
quadraticsGraph.tip: Use the Quadratic Explorer simulation to see how a, b, c change the graph.

vertexForm.name: Vertex form (completing the square)
vertexForm.rule: y = a(x − h)² + k. Vertex at (h, k). Axis of symmetry: x = h. Convert from standard form by completing the square.
vertexForm.tip: Half the coefficient of x, square it, add and subtract. Vertex form makes maxima/minima trivial to read.

transformations.name: Function transformations
transformations.rule: y = f(x) + a — shift UP by a. y = f(x + a) — shift LEFT by a. y = af(x) — vertical stretch by a. y = f(ax) — horizontal compression by a. y = −f(x) — reflect in x-axis. y = f(−x) — reflect in y-axis.
examples: y = (x − 3)² + 2 is y = x² shifted right 3, up 2.
y = 2x² is y = x² stretched vertically by 2.
y = (2x)² = 4x² is y = x² compressed horizontally by 2 — same as vertical stretch by 4.
y = −sin(x) is y = sin(x) reflected in x-axis.
transformations.watchOut: Horizontal shift sign is OPPOSITE to what you write: f(x − 3) shifts RIGHT 3 (not left). Counter-intuitive.

solveGeneral.name: Solving quadratics — general
solveGeneral.rule: ax² + bx + c = 0. Four methods: 1) factorize, 2) complete the square, 3) quadratic formula, 4) graphically. Always check: discriminant Δ = b² − 4ac. Δ > 0: 2 real roots; Δ = 0: 1 root (repeated); Δ < 0: no real roots.
examples: x² + x + 1 = 0 → Δ = −3 < 0 → no real solutions.
solveGeneral.tip: If factorizing fails quickly, use the formula. Discriminant tells you what to expect BEFORE solving.

solveFactor.name: Solving — factorize
solveFactor.rule: If product of factors = 0, then at least one factor = 0. (x − a)(x − b) = 0 ⇒ x = a or x = b. Fastest method when factors are integers.
solveFactor.watchOut: Don't divide by x — you lose the x = 0 solution. Move all terms to one side first.

solveCompleteSquare.name: Solving — complete the square
solveCompleteSquare.rule: Convert to vertex form, then isolate the square. (x + p)² = q ⇒ x = −p ± √q. Useful when factoring fails and you want exact (surd) answers.
solveCompleteSquare.tip: Always works for x² coefficient = 1. For a ≠ 1, factor a out first.

solveFormula.name: Solving — quadratic formula
solveFormula.rule: For ax² + bx + c = 0, x = [−b ± √(b² − 4ac)] / 2a. Works for ALL quadratics, gives exact answers in surd form.
solveFormula.tip: Memorise: 'x equals minus b plus or minus square root of b squared minus four a c, all over two a'. Always works.

solveGraphical.name: Solving — graphically
solveGraphical.rule: x-intercepts of y = f(x) are the solutions of f(x) = 0. Intersection of y = f(x) and y = g(x) gives solutions of f(x) = g(x).
examples: x² − 4 = 0 → graph y = x² − 4, read x = ±2 where graph crosses x-axis.
Solve x² = x + 2 by plotting y = x² and y = x + 2 — intersect at x = −1, x = 2.
solveGraphical.tip: Use GDC/Desmos. Useful when algebra is hard, or you only need an approximate answer.

rearrange.name: Changing the subject of a formula
rearrange.rule: Treat variables like numbers. Undo operations in reverse order. Whatever you do to one side, do to the other.
examples: Make r the subject of A = πr². → r² = A/π → r = √(A/π).
Make t the subject of v = u + at. → t = (v − u)/a.
Make h the subject of V = πr²h/3 → h = 3V/(πr²).
Make x the subject of y = (x+1)/(x−2). → y(x−2) = x+1 → yx − 2y = x + 1 → x(y−1) = 1 + 2y → x = (1+2y)/(y−1).
rearrange.tip: When the variable appears twice, collect terms with it on one side and factorize it out.

algebraicFractions.name: Algebraic fractions (Extended)
algebraicFractions.rule: Same rules as numeric fractions: common denominator to add/subtract; multiply numerators × numerators, denominators × denominators; divide by multiplying by the reciprocal. Always factorize first.
algebraicFractions.watchOut: Note excluded values where denominator = 0. Cancel only FACTORS, not terms.

proportions.name: Direct & inverse proportions
proportions.rule: Direct: y = kx (y ∝ x); doubling x doubles y. Inverse: y = k/x (y ∝ 1/x); doubling x halves y. Can have squares/cubes too: y ∝ x² or y ∝ 1/x³.
examples: Cost ∝ weight. 3 kg cost £6 → k = 2, so 5 kg cost £10.
Time ∝ 1/speed. At 60 km/h trip takes 4h → k=240, so at 80 km/h takes 240/80 = 3h.
proportions.tip: Step 1: write the proportion as y = kx (or y = k/x). Step 2: use given pair to find k. Step 3: apply to new value.

asymptotes.name: Rational functions — asymptotes
asymptotes.rule: y = 1/x has vertical asymptote x = 0 and horizontal asymptote y = 0. Vertical: where denominator = 0 (and numerator ≠ 0). Horizontal: limit as x → ±∞.
examples: y = 1/(x − 3) → vertical asymptote x = 3, horizontal y = 0.
y = (x+1)/(x−2) → vertical x = 2, horizontal y = 1 (ratio of leading coefficients).
y = 1/x²: VA x = 0, HA y = 0, always positive — different from 1/x.
asymptotes.tip: Hyperbola y = k/x has two branches in opposite quadrants. Graph never touches the asymptotes.

exponential.name: Exponential functions
exponential.rule: y = aᵇˣ where a > 0, b > 0, b ≠ 1. b > 1: growth (rapidly increasing). 0 < b < 1: decay (rapidly decreasing to 0). Passes through (0, a).
examples: y = 2ˣ: doubles every unit. y(0)=1, y(3)=8.
Bacterial growth: N = N₀ · 2^(t/T) where T is doubling time.
exponential.tip: Exponential ≠ polynomial. Compare 2ˣ vs x² — exponential WINS for large x. Always positive, never zero.

sequences.title: Sequences & Series
sequences.intro: Linear, quadratic and geometric sequences. General rule for the nth term; sum to infinity (Extended).

linearGeneral.name: Linear (arithmetic) sequences — general rule
linearGeneral.rule: nth term: uₙ = a + (n − 1)d, where a = first term, d = common difference. The common difference is constant — each term increases (or decreases) by the same amount.
examples: What's the 100th term of 7, 12, 17, …? a=7, d=5, u₁₀₀ = 7 + 99·5 = 502.
linearGeneral.tip: Always check: first term (sub n=1) gives a. Sum of first n terms: Sₙ = n/2 · (2a + (n−1)d) = n/2 · (a + uₙ).

linearProblems.name: Linear sequences — problem solving
linearProblems.rule: Use uₙ formula to: find a missing term; find which term has a given value (solve for n); find d given two non-consecutive terms.
examples: The 5th term is 22, the 10th term is 47. Find a and d. → u₁₀ − u₅ = 5d → 47 − 22 = 25 → d = 5. Then a = 22 − 4·5 = 2.
Which term equals 200 in 3, 7, 11, …? d=4, a=3. 200 = 3 + 4(n−1) → 197 = 4(n−1) → n = 50.25 → not a term (must be integer).
linearProblems.tip: If n isn't an integer, the value isn't in the sequence. Watch for 'how many terms are less than X' type questions.

geometric.name: Geometric sequences — general rule
geometric.rule: nth term: uₙ = a · r^(n−1), where a = first term, r = common RATIO. Each term is multiplied by r to get the next.
examples: Compound interest: £500 at 4% pa: 500, 520, 540.8, … → r = 1.04. After n years: 500·1.04ⁿ.
geometric.tip: r is the RATIO (divide successive terms): uₙ₊₁/uₙ = r. If r > 1: grows. 0 < r < 1: decays. r < 0: alternates sign.

sumInfinity.name: Sum to infinity (Extended)
sumInfinity.rule: For a geometric series with |r| < 1, the sum to infinity converges: S∞ = a / (1 − r). If |r| ≥ 1, sum diverges (no finite value).
sumInfinity.watchOut: Only converges if |r| < 1. For r = 1, sum = na (linear, infinite). For r ≥ 1, infinite.

geometryTrig.title: Geometry & Trigonometry
geometryTrig.intro: Pythagoras, trig ratios, sine and cosine rules, circle theorems, sectors and arcs.

pythagoras.name: Pythagoras (incl. converse — Extended)
pythagoras.rule: In a right-angled triangle: a² + b² = c², where c is the hypotenuse (longest side, opposite right angle). Converse: if a² + b² = c², the triangle is right-angled.
examples: Is a triangle with sides 7, 24, 25 right-angled? 7² + 24² = 625 = 25² → YES.
3D Pythagoras: diagonal of a box a×b×c = √(a² + b² + c²).
pythagoras.tip: Common Pythagorean triples: (3,4,5), (5,12,13), (8,15,17), (7,24,25). Always identify the hypotenuse first.

trig.name: Trigonometry (SOHCAHTOA)
trig.rule: In right-angled triangles: sin θ = Opp/Hyp; cos θ = Adj/Hyp; tan θ = Opp/Adj. Use inverse (sin⁻¹, cos⁻¹, tan⁻¹) to find an angle from a ratio.
examples: Find x: hyp = 10, adj = 6, θ = ? → cos θ = 6/10 → θ = cos⁻¹(0.6) ≈ 53.1°.
Find side: opp = ?, hyp = 8, θ = 30° → opp = 8·sin 30° = 4.
Tower 50 m. Angle of elevation from 80 m away: tan θ = 50/80 → θ ≈ 32°.
Angle of depression = angle of elevation (alternate angles).
trig.tip: Label sides relative to the angle you know/need. Hypotenuse is always opposite the right angle.

bearings.name: Bearings
bearings.rule: Three-figure clockwise from North. Always 000° to 360°. Written 045°, 120°, 270° etc. North = 000°. East = 090°. South = 180°. West = 270°.
examples: Bearing of B from A is 060°. Bearing of A from B: 060° + 180° = 240°.
From P, Q is at bearing 110° at 5 km. Find Q's coordinates if P at origin: Δx = 5 sin 110°, Δy = 5 cos 110°.
Back bearings: if forward is θ, back is θ + 180° (or − 180° if θ > 180°).
bearings.tip: Always draw a diagram. Three figures: 6° not 'six degrees' but '006°'. Direction is from observer.

sineCosine.name: Sine & Cosine rules (Extended)
sineCosine.rule: Sine rule: a/sin A = b/sin B = c/sin C (for non-right triangles). Cosine rule: a² = b² + c² − 2bc·cos A. Use sine for AAS, ASA; cosine for SAS, SSS.
examples: Sine rule (AAS): angles 40°, 60°, side opp 60° = 8. Side opp 40°: 8 sin 40° / sin 60° ≈ 5.94.
Cosine rule (SAS): sides 7 and 9, included angle 50°. Third side: √(49 + 81 − 2·7·9·cos 50°) ≈ 6.93.
sineCosine.tip: Ambiguous case (SSA) with sine rule: sin θ may give two valid angles (θ or 180°−θ). Check with triangle context.

triangleArea.name: Area using ½ab·sin(C)
triangleArea.rule: For any triangle with sides a, b and included angle C: Area = ½ · a · b · sin C. Useful when base/height aren't given.
examples: Sides 6 cm and 8 cm, angle between them 30°: Area = ½·6·8·sin 30° = 12 cm².
Compare with traditional Area = ½ base · height — same answer but ½ab sin C doesn't need height.
triangleArea.tip: C must be the angle BETWEEN sides a and b. Works for all triangles, not just right-angled.

circleTheorems.name: Circle theorems
circleTheorems.rule: Key: (1) Angle at centre = 2× angle at circumference (same arc). (2) Angle in semicircle = 90°. (3) Angles in same segment = equal. (4) Cyclic quad opposite angles sum to 180°. (5) Tangent ⊥ radius at point of contact. (6) Tangents from same external point are equal length. (7) Alternate segment theorem.
examples: Angle at centre 80° → angle at circumference (same arc) = 40°.
Triangle inscribed in semicircle with diameter as one side: opposite angle = 90°.
circleTheorems.tip: Always label diagram. Look for 'same arc' or 'same chord' as a trigger to apply a theorem.

sectors.name: Sectors and arcs
sectors.rule: Arc length L = (θ/360°) · 2πr. Sector area A = (θ/360°) · πr². (θ in degrees; in radians multiply by r and r²/2 respectively.)
examples: If arc length = 10 and r = 4, then θ = (10/(2π·4)) · 360° ≈ 143.2°.
sectors.tip: Don't confuse sector (slice) with segment (between chord & arc). Always check θ is in DEGREES if formula uses /360.

segments.name: Area of a segment
segments.rule: Segment area = Sector area − Triangle area = (θ/360°)·πr² − ½r²·sin θ. The 'pizza crust' between chord and arc.
examples: r = 6, θ = 90°: sector = π·36/4 = 9π. Triangle = ½·6·6·sin 90° = 18. Segment = 9π − 18 ≈ 10.27.
segments.tip: For small θ, segment is thin. For θ = 180° (semicircle), 'segment' = semicircle (triangle area = 0).

intersectingChords.name: Intersecting chords
intersectingChords.rule: If two chords intersect inside a circle at point P: AP · PB = CP · PD (product of segments on each chord is equal). Power of a point theorem.
examples: Chord AB and chord CD cross at P. AP = 6, PB = 4, CP = 3. Find PD: 6·4 = 3·PD → PD = 8.
If P is OUTSIDE circle with two secants: PA·PB = PC·PD (still products of full distances from P to far intersection times near).
intersectingChords.tip: Always identify which segments. Inside circle vs outside: both have product-equals-product, but distances differ.

statsProb.title: Statistics & Probability
statsProb.intro: Histograms, box plots, stem-and-leaf plots, grouped data, cumulative frequency, standard deviation (Extended).

representation.name: Data representation — choosing the right chart
representation.rule: Match the chart to the data type. Categorical: bar chart, pie chart. Numerical: histogram (continuous), bar chart (discrete). Comparing groups: box plots side-by-side. Showing change over time: line graph. Relationships: scatter graph.
examples: Favourite ice cream flavour → bar chart (categorical).
Heights of students → histogram (continuous data, grouped).
Goals scored per match → bar chart (discrete count).
Temperature vs ice cream sales → scatter graph.
representation.tip: Histograms are NOT bar charts: bars touch (continuous), and area = frequency (not height in unequal classes).

stemLeaf.name: Stem and leaf diagram
stemLeaf.rule: Split each number into 'stem' (tens) and 'leaf' (units). Keeps all data values visible while showing distribution. Always include a KEY.
examples: Back-to-back stem-leaf compares two groups: e.g., boys vs girls heights with shared stems in the middle.
stemLeaf.tip: Leaves must be in ASCENDING order. Always include key — examiners deduct marks for missing it.

grouped.name: Grouped data — mean, median, mode
grouped.rule: From a frequency table: mean ≈ Σ(midpoint × frequency) / Σ frequency. Mode = class with highest frequency. Median = middle value, found in the cumulative frequency class.
examples: Median position = 20/2 = 10th value → falls in 10–20 class.
grouped.tip: Use class midpoints, not class boundaries, for mean. State 'modal class', not 'mode' for grouped data.

boxPlots.name: Box plots (box-and-whisker)
boxPlots.rule: Five-number summary: min, Q1 (25%), median (50%), Q3 (75%), max. Box spans Q1 to Q3 (IQR = Q3 − Q1). Whiskers reach min/max. Outliers if outside 1.5·IQR.
examples: Comparing: 'Group A has higher median and larger IQR — more variable than Group B'.
boxPlots.tip: IQR = Q3 − Q1 is a measure of spread that ignores outliers. Length of box = IQR; shorter = more consistent.

histograms.name: Histograms (incl. unequal class widths)
histograms.rule: Continuous data, bars TOUCH. For unequal classes: y-axis = frequency density = frequency / class width. Then AREA = frequency.
examples: Classes: 0–10 (freq 20, width 10, density 2). 10–30 (freq 60, width 20, density 3). 30–60 (freq 30, width 30, density 1).
Total frequency = sum of (density × width). Check: 2·10 + 3·20 + 1·30 = 110.
histograms.watchOut: For equal class widths, y-axis can simply be frequency. For UNEQUAL widths it MUST be frequency density. Common mistake.

cumulativeFreq.name: Cumulative frequency (incl. percentiles)
cumulativeFreq.rule: Running total of frequencies. Plot CF against UPPER bound of each class. Curve is S-shaped. Read off: median (50%), Q1 (25%), Q3 (75%), or any percentile.
examples: Median at CF = 10 → x ≈ 16. Q1 at CF = 5 → x ≈ 11. Q3 at CF = 15 → x ≈ 21.
cumulativeFreq.tip: Always plot CF against UPPER class boundary. Curve passes through (lower bound, 0).

stdDev.name: Standard deviation (Extended)
stdDev.rule: σ measures the spread of data around the mean. Formula: σ = √[Σ(x − x̄)² / n]. Larger σ = more spread. ≈ 68% of data within 1σ of mean (Normal distribution).
examples: Compare: data 4.5, 5, 5, 5, 5, 5.5 has same mean 5, but σ ≈ 0.32. Much more consistent.
stdDev.tip: σ is in the same UNITS as the data. σ² (variance) is in units². Standard deviation gives a sense of typical distance from mean.

pieCharts.name: Pie charts
pieCharts.rule: Angle for each category = (frequency / total) × 360°. Must sum to 360°. Best for showing proportions of a whole, ≤6 categories.
examples: Survey of 60 people: 20 walk, 10 cycle, 30 drive. Angles: walk 120°, cycle 60°, drive 180°. Total 360° ✓.
If sector for 'cycle' = 30° in pie of 240 students, count = (30/360)·240 = 20 cyclists.
pieCharts.tip: Avoid for: >6 categories (cluttered), comparing exact values (use bar chart). Always label or include a key.

eoyPractice.title: EOY Practice
eoyPractice.intro: AssessPrep e-assessment codes: GD29PC and Y9636Y. Review-in-Context questions from the textbooks below are an effective rehearsal in the same IB MYP style.

criteriaTrainer.title: Criteria Trainer
criteriaTrainer.intro: Practice in the format of MYP Criteria A, B, C and D.

criterionA.title: Criterion A — Knowing & Understanding
criterionA.description: Short calculation tasks across all five topics.

criterionB.title: Criterion B — Investigating Patterns
criterionB.description: Spot the pattern → general rule → verify → justify.

criterionD.title: Criterion D — Applying to Real Life
criterionD.description: Real-world problem solving — identify factors, calculate, justify, evaluate.

standardForm.title: Standard Form Converter
standardForm.description: Convert between standard form a × 10ⁿ and ordinary notation.

quadraticExplorer.title: Quadratic Explorer
quadraticExplorer.description: Slide a, b, c to see the parabola, vertex, axis of symmetry and roots update live.

sequenceGenerator.title: Sequence Generator
sequenceGenerator.description: Generate linear or geometric sequences, view terms and the n-th term formula.

sectorSegment.title: Sector & Segment Calculator
sectorSegment.description: Vary radius and angle to compute sector / segment area.

boxPlot.title: Box Plot Builder
boxPlot.description: Paste a dataset and see the box plot with quartiles and outliers.

surdSimplifier.title: Surd Simplifier
surdSimplifier.description: Extract perfect square factors to simplify any √n.

transformations.title: Function Transformations
transformations.description: See how a·f(x − h) + k transforms a base function.

quadraticSolver.title: Quadratic Equation Solver
quadraticSolver.description: See all three methods (formula, completing the square, factorisation) side by side.

rightTriangle.title: Right-Angled Triangle Trainer
rightTriangle.description: Drag the legs; see hypotenuse and angles computed from Pythagoras and trig.

numberSetsVenn.title: Number Sets — Venn
numberSetsVenn.description: Type any number (5, -3, 1/2, √2, π) — see which sets ℕ, ℤ, ℚ, ℝ it belongs to.

bearingsCompass.title: Bearings Compass
bearingsCompass.description: Vary bearing and distance — see the path on a compass and the N/E offsets.

cumulativeFreq.title: Cumulative Frequency Curve
cumulativeFreq.description: Edit class frequencies. Move the percentile slider to read the value at Q1, median, Q3 or any %.

circleTheorems.title: Circle Theorems Playground
circleTheorems.description: Four key theorems with draggable points: centre = 2× circumference, semicircle 90°, cyclic quadrilateral, intersecting chords.
